coredata.dc:title: Luminescent polymer composite made of glucose derived carbon dots/polyvinyl alcohol for the sensitive detection of Pb2+ in water
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Ravi, Soumya
Jayaraj, M.K
coredata.dc:description: The present work emphasizes on the properties of luminescent polymer composites made from glucose derived carbon dots (g-CDs) integrated into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix by a solution casting method and their application for the trace level detection of Pb2+ in water based on fluorescence quenching. This method offers a convenient approach to explore solid state fluorescence of CDs in a host matrix providing good chemical, thermal and mechanical stability to CDs and envisages on the development of flexible, freestanding and sustainable polymer films as probes for the on-site strip based testing of lead in water. The emission wavelength appeared to be broad covering almost the entire visible spectrum, whereas the g-CDs showed emission only in the green region of the visible spectrum. The photoluminescence (PL) was also unaffected by the change in pH levels in aqueous media providing ample scope for their application in both acidic and basic conditions. The composites are found to be environmentally benign as confirmed from cytotoxicity studies and exhibit fluorescence quenching which correlates with the concentration of Pb2+ ions. The detection limit is as low as 4.71 nM and 0.93 nM respectively for g-CDs and g-CDs/PVA composite, the lowest values reported for sugar derived CDs and its polymer composite. This sustainable probe shows good linearity in the low detection range 0–50 nM and the experimental results confirm a static quenching process for the selective and sensitive detection of Pb2+.

head.ce:title: Luminescent polymer composite made of glucose derived carbon dots/polyvinyl alcohol for the sensitive detection of Pb2+ in water

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present work emphasizes on the properties of luminescent polymer composites made from glucose derived carbon dots (g-CDs) integrated into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix by a solution casting method and their application for the trace level detection of Pb2+ in water based on fluorescence quenching. This method offers a convenient approach to explore solid state fluorescence of CDs in a host matrix providing good chemical, thermal and mechanical stability to CDs and envisages on the development of flexible, freestanding and sustainable polymer films as probes for the on-site strip based testing of lead in water. The emission wavelength appeared to be broad covering almost the entire visible spectrum, whereas the g-CDs showed emission only in the green region of the visible spectrum. The photoluminescence (PL) was also unaffected by the change in pH levels in aqueous media providing ample scope for their application in both acidic and basic conditions. The composites are found to be environmentally benign as confirmed from cytotoxicity studies and exhibit fluorescence quenching which correlates with the concentration of Pb2+ ions. The detection limit is as low as 4.71 nM and 0.93 nM respectively for g-CDs and g-CDs/PVA composite, the lowest values reported for sugar derived CDs and its polymer composite. This sustainable probe shows good linearity in the low detection range 0–50 nM and the experimental results confirm a static quenching process for the selective and sensitive detection of Pb2+.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanoparticles of carbon having quasi-spherical shape and size roughly in the range of 10 nm or less known as carbon dots (CDs) are considered suitable alternatives to the conventional inorganic semiconductor quantum dots owing to their unique properties such as robust photoluminescence, excellent biocompatibility, high photo-bleaching resistivity and chemical stability to exist in the colloidal solution state[1–3]. They are found to be excellent tools in bio-imaging, catalysis, chemical sensing, and optoelectronic applications [4–8]. CDs are a generalized name assigned to the broad category of carbon nanoparticles that encompass graphene quantum dots (GQDs) having a single or double layered graphitic plane with surface functional groups or interlayer defects; carbon nanodots having spherical cores with multiple graphitic layers and edge functionalities and polymer dots(PDs) which have a carbon core which is either graphitized or cross-linked with surface groups attached to them. Different strategies of top-down and bottom-up methods have been adopted to synthesize CDs. Top-down approaches deal with the conversion of graphite based starting materials like carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, coal via processes like chemical oxidative cutting, electron beam lithography, laser ablation, chemical oxidation, and carving of carbon fiber, electrochemical synthesis, etc.[9–13]. Bottom-up approaches include developing CDs from small molecules like citric acid, L-cysteine, carbohydrates, biomaterials, etc. via microwave heating, solvothermal/ hydrothermal processes which more or less allow step-wise synthesis and hence precise control over the size and morphology could be ensured[14,15]. Simple alternative methods like carbonization of organic precursors like carbohydrates; waste materials etc. by thermal treatment in alkaline/acid medium are also adopted. For most of the applications, CDs are employed either in the solution phase or in powder form which limits their application in the former case and results in the agglomeration of the particles in the latter case. The merits of CDs compared with conventional semiconductor quantum dots and fluorescent dyes made of toxic heavy metal elements widen their application in bio‐imaging and photocatalysis [16,17]. Nanomaterials free from health and environmental hazards are highly preferred for multifarious technological applications. At this juncture, carbon‐based fluorescent quantum dots are the best alternatives in terms of biocompatibility and reduced toxicity. Embedding CDs into a polymer matrix is an appropriate method to protect them from the working environment and to explore new characteristics and applications of resultant composites [18,19]. Due to the abundance of oxygen/hydrogen-containing functionalities such as –OH and –COOH on the surfaces of CDs, they can be easily integrated into the polymer matrix. Polymers not only serve the role of a matrix but also provide excellent chemical and mechanical stability to the nanocomposites which favor their utilization as technologically relevant structures such as flexible freestanding films. Combining CDs and polymers to form new composites with unique functionalities has been reported recently. Introducing nitrogen-doped CDs into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to tune the refractive index of CDs-PVA composite was reported by Kumar et al [20]. Wu et al. and Yang et al. explored the shape memory effects of CDs/PVA nanocomposite films [21,22]. Saheeda et al. studied the pH independent fluorescent properties of CNT-derived CDs impregnated into PVA matrix [23]. It is interesting to note that PVA is the most preferable polymer matrix chosen because of its excellent water solubility which goes in tune with CDs, good optical transparency, high chemical resistance, easy processability, and exceptional film-forming properties. In addition, they are also environmentally benign in terms of biocompatibility, biodegradability, and non-toxic nature.

The use of CDs as fluorescent probes or optical sensors for the trace detection of heavy metal pollutants in water has gained attention in recent times due to the fact that they are rapid, simple and efficient[24,25]. Most of the analytes such as metal ions are reported to cause a change in the fluorescence of CDs which occur either by a direct interaction between analyte ion and CDs or by CDs that are functionalized with several molecules to detect the target ions. The analytes either attenuate the fluorescence intensity by quenching or enhance the intensity by suppressing the quenching effect [26,27]. Majority of the reported processes cause quenching of fluorescence intensity due to electrostatic interaction between the surface of CDs which is negatively charged and the positively charged metal ions. The several processes include static quenching effect (SQE) via a ground state complex formation[28], dynamic quenching effect (DQE) via collisional interactions that lead to charge/energy transfer[29], inner filter effect (IFE) due to the absorption of emitted light by the analyte ion-CD complex [30], resonance energy transfer (FRET) which happens when there is an overlap between the the emission spectra of CDs (donor) and the absorption spectra of the analyte ions (acceptor) [31], photo-induced electron transfer(PET) in which electron transfer happen from CD to the metal ion to form a complex in the excited state which decays to lower state by non-radiative transition [32].

Among the various pollutants, the pollution of water bodies with Lead from industrial effluents continues to be a serious concern to aquatic and human life. Owing to its toxic and carcinogenic effect, the U.S Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has limited the permissible amount of lead in drinking water to be 15 µg/L (15 ppb, ∼ 72 nM). The feasibility of CDs for the selective detection of Pb2+ is reported in literature since Pb2+ is found to have good affinity for the –OH and – COOH functional groups present in CDs[33–35]. For instance, Nandi et al. developed CDs from a biological media potato-dextrose agar (PDA) via microwave treatment for the detection of Pb2+ with detection limit (LOD) as low as 0.1 nM but a detailed analysis on the sensing mechanism was absent [36]. N-doped CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal treatment of Lantana camara berries in the detection range 0–200 nM and LOD 9.64 nM and the mechanism of PL quenching was dynamic [37]. Kala et al. reported the Pb2+ detection by GQDs modified by nitrogen functionalized dithienopyrrole (DTP-PPD) in the linear range 3–30 nM with a LOD of 1.02 nM [38]. Most of these studies are restricted to the use of g-CDs in solution phase which cause aggregation and precipitation over time which affects their stability and limits real time application. However, solid state sensors like paper based sensors, membrane based strips, and gel substrates eliminate the use of solvents and reagents etc. and are highly preferred for better chemical stability and fast response. The use of a fluorescent film based sensor based on S, N-doped CDs integrated in PVA matrix was reported recently for the detection of Cr (VI) and ascorbic acid in the micromolar region [39]. Studies with regard to the detection of Pb2+ using fluorescent film based sensors are rarely reported as of now.

The focal theme of the present work is that sustainable CDs derived from a benign precursor glucose (g-CDs) synthesized via a simple heating method are embedded into PVA matrix to obtain flexible nanocomposite films by a facile solution casting method with interesting photoluminescence properties. Their pH dependent variation in the PL emission is demonstrated which showed that despite variations in pH, the PL emission was unaffected when integrated into polymer matrix unlike in the case of pristine CDs whose PL intensity varies considerably in acidic and basic environments. The feasibility of g-CDs/PVA composite as a sustainable probe for the trace level detection of heavy metal ion lead is explored where in PL quenching is observed with increasing concentration of Pb2+ with detection limit as low as 0.93 nM. The use of such a film based handy, ultra-fast sensors provide ample scope for on-site strip based detection and testing of Pb2+ and in the environmental monitoring of heavy metal ion pollution in water.

D-glucose used belongs to Merck Life Sciences Pvt. Ltd. PVA, (MW = 140 000, 98–99 % hydrolysed) was purchased from HIMEDIA Laboratories Pvt. Ltd (India). Sulphuric acid (H2SO4, purity > 98 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), metal salts like lead acetate, cobalt chloride, nickel acetate etc. were procured from Merck Life Sciences Pvt. Ltd and were used as received. All chemicals used were of analytical grade. Deionized water was used throughout the experiment for synthesis and washing purposes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of g-CDs and fabrication of g-CD/PVA nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized from D-glucose by a facile heating method according to the previously reported procedure [24]. Precisely, 20 g of D-glucose was initially heated at 80 °C for 30 min to initiate caramelization of glucose, and then raised to 180 °C until color changed to deep brown, indicating complete caramelization. To aid further dehydration, 1 M H2SO4 was added to the caramelized glucose. The solution was washed thoroughly with de-ionized water, centrifuged at 12000 rpm; the supernatant was taken out, syringe-filtered and ultrasonicated for an hour. A pH of 5 was observed and the solution was then freeze-dried and stored. The final yield of freeze dried g-CDs was about 5g.

In order to fabricate the g-CD/PVA composite film, 600 mg of PVA was dissolved in 10 ml distilled water by continuous magnetic stirring at 80 °C for 2 h to completely dissolve the polymer get a homogeneous solution. Various concentrations of g-CDs were added to the polymer solution to constitute different weight percentages of g-CDs in PVA solution (For instance, 18 mg of g-CDs for 3 wt%, 30 mg for 5 wt%, 60 mg for 10 wt%, 72 mg for 12 wt%, 90 mg for 15 wt% and 120 mg for 20 wt%). g-CDs dissolved almost instantaneously. The solution was then poured into a Teflon lined petridish and dried at 70 °C for 6 h and film formation occurs with the evaporation of water to obtain flexible, freestanding composite films which could be peeled off when dried. The films thus obtained were stored under vacuum in a desiccator at room temperature. Fig. 1 a) depicts the various stages of g-CDs synthesis and the fabrication of flexible, freestanding g-CD/PVA composite films. Fig. 1b) represent a cross section of the composite film illustrating the anchoring of g-CDs in such a way that it occupy the free volume within the amorphous domains of the PVA matrix which also has semi-crystalline domains.

Standard MTT assay [40] was used to study the cell viability of g-CDs/PVA composite. The neurotoxicity effects were determined by treating fast-growing mouse neuroblastoma cell line known as N2a cells with the composite sample for 48 h followed by MTT assay.

The stock solution of Pb2+ was prepared for different concentrations (250 µM to 5 nM) by serial dilution of Lead acetate with de-ionized water. For the detection of Pb2+, 1 ml of g-CDs solution (20 mg/ml) was added to 3 ml of stock solution and mixed well in the cuvette without further incubation. Subsequently, the emission spectra for each concentration were recorded. The same procedure was adopted when various metal ions were added and interference experiments were performed. In the case of g-CDs/PVA composite films, 0.5 ml of varying concentration of lead solution was drop wise added homogenously on to films, dried and PL recorded. All the experiments were conducted in triplicates.

The morphological analysis of the g-CDs was performed using high resolution transmission electron microscopy HRTEM (JEOL JEM 2100 with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV). The structural properties were established using Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR-Shimadzu IR Affinity). Optical absorption spectra of the nanocomposite films were recorded on a JASCO‐V570 spectrophotometer in the wavelength range from 200 nm to 700 nm. PL emission studies, quantum yield (QY) and lifetime of g-CDs, lead added g-CDs and the composite samples were performed using spectrofluorometer HORIBA PTI QM 8075–11-C which has a 150 W Xenon lamp as the excitation source and an integrating sphere attached to the fluorometer for QY measurements. The thermal properties of the samples were performed using HITACHI-STA7300 simultaneous thermogravimetric analyzer. The stress–strain studies to understand the mechanical characteristics of the composites were performed on Universal Testing Machine (UTM- DAK System Inc. Model-T-72052 Hi-Tech). The load cell was at 0.2kN. The thickness and width were in the range 0.09–0.1 mm and 10–12 mm respectively. The Raman spectra of the samples were recorded using LabRam HR micro-Raman spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon).

The g-CDs formation happens through a bottom-up approach initiated with the caramelization of glucose followed by dehydration in the presence of concentrated H2SO4. The caramelization of sugars is understood to be a complex process and involves a series of chemical reactions that happen simultaneously. Caramelization is usually kicked off at 160 °C and the decomposition pathways involve the formation of aromatic reaction intermediates [41]. The addition of strong acid H2SO4 aids the dehydration process and result in the condensation and aromatizing of intermediates to generate carbon clusters comprising of carbonyl moieties C = C as heating proceeds. More and more carbonyl moieties are generated in size and the functional groups like carboxyl and hydroxyl originating from the reaction intermediates gets attached on to the surface of carbon clusters which grow in size and exceed a supercritical saturation level resulting in the formation of g-CDs decorated with oxygen rich functional groups (Fig. S1b). By centrifugation and filtering, larger carbon particles are filtered off to yield g-CDs which are ultrasonicated further for analysis. Fig. 2 a) shows the HR-TEM image of the g-CDs derived from glucose and exhibits monodispersed particles having a size less than 10 nm with an average size of about 6 nm (Fig. 2b). The high resolution image as given in the inset of Fig. 2a) indicates an interplanar spacing of 0.33 nm corresponding to the (002) graphitic plane indicating that g-CDs are mainly composed of carbon core with aromatic sp2 domains [42,43]. Fig. 2(c–d) shows the distribution of g-CDs in PVA matrix at different g-CDs loadings. At lower weight percentages of g-CDs, there is more homogenous distribution of g-CDs and less aggregation as observed from Fig. 2c). Small aggregrations with size less than 30 nm are observed patchily. At higher g-CD loading of 20 wt%, large agglomerates of size more than 100 nm are observed due to the aggregation of g-CDs in PVA as seen in Fig. 2d). g-CDs exhibited good dispersibility in PVA matrix without any surface modification unlike conventional inorganic QDs and metallic nanoparticles which require additional surface treatments to prevent aggregation. The presence of surface functional groups in g-CDs and interaction of g-CDs with PVA in the composites is established through FTIR spectra as provided in Fig. 3 . The intense broad band in the range 3300–3400 cm−1 visible in PVA, g-CDs, and the composites correspond to the –OH stretching vibration mode of hydroxyl group present in all samples [44]. The absorption band at 2880–2900 cm−1 tells us about the asymmetric stretching mode of the aliphatic C-H group which is more evident in the case of g-CDs and the composite with higher g-CDs content. The band at ∼ 1700 cm-1 is characteristic of C = O stretching mode of the carboxylate group (–COOH) present in g-CDs. In the case of PVA, it arises from the hydrolysis of polyvinyl acetate to form PVA [45]. The asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrational modes of COO– groups are manifested as absorption bands at 1630 cm−1 and 1423 cm−1 respectively. The sharp band at 1040 cm−1 is characteristic of the stretching vibration of the C-O-C functional group [44]. The absorption bands observed in the spectrum of g-CDs are also evident in composites though the slight shift in the intensity and peak position is observed in some of the bands. These observations are indicative of the strong anchoring of g-CDs in the PVA matrix.

The optical properties of the g-CD/PVA nanocomposite films were elucidated using UV–visible absorption, transmittance and photoluminescence (PL) emission spectroscopy. Fig. 4 explains the UV–vis absorption and transmittance spectra of g-CDs and the composite films. The UV–vis absorption spectra of g-CDs at various concentrations are shown in Fig. 4a). An absorption peak centered at around 275 nm corresponds to the π–π* transition of the conjugated C = C bonds in the aromatic sp2 domains of the carbon core. The absorption tail spans in the visible region and a weak shoulder peak observed at higher wavelengths above 300 nm could be attributed to the n-π* transition of the carbonyl functional groups attached to the surface of the g-CDs[46]. Most apparent peaks were obtained for a g-CDs concentration of 15 mg/ml. Fig. 4b) summarizes the comparison of the absorption properties of the g-CDs (15 mg/ml) and the composites in the solution and the membrane state (10 wt% g-CDs). As is evident, the absorption features of g-CDs and the composite in the solution phase slightly change when solid composite film formation takes place. The peaks become obvious and sharp. A slight blue shift is observed from the peak at 281 nm to 277 nm while the shoulder peak at ∼ 350 nm does not shift. The slight shift might be attributed to the change in the π–π* electronic transition induced due to the alterations in the surface edge states caused by the bonding between PVA and surface functional groups of g-CDs. The dependence of absorption and emission positions on the surface environment is studied recently for CDs. Pramanik et al.[47] and Mukherjee et al. [48] observed absorption redshifts CDs in protic solvents and these shifts were ascribed to the hydrogen bonding that stabilizes the excited states of surface functional groups like –NH, –OH, etc. Similar shifts in the absorption peaks were also reported for CDs in solvents having high hydrogen bonding capability [49]. These CDs have emission centers originating from the surface fluorophore and peripheral edge states. In our study, the shoulder peak at 350 nm was similar for g-CDs in the solution phase and in g-CD/PVA composite film which indicates that similar hydrogen bonding occurs between the g-CDs and –OH group of PVA as the hydrogen bond of water with CDs. The UV–vis transmittance spectra and the optical transparency of the composite samples are shown in Fig. 4c) and d) respectively. The thickness of the composite films was about 80–100 µm. As the g-CDs concentration increases in the polymer matrix, the transmittance decreases considerably. The dependence of the film’s optical transparency (light transmittance at 500 nm) on the g-CD concentration as shown in Fig. 4d) also exhibits a declining trend. At lower weight percentages of g-CDs, there is proper nanoscale dispersion of g-CDs in the matrix which accounts for high optical transparency above 50 %. As the g-CDs content increases, there is agglomeration which deteriorates the light transmittance. This is also backed by the TEM observations discussed previously. The photoluminescence spectra of the samples are demonstrated in Fig. 5 . g-CDs show an emission peak centered at around 520 nm when excited at 410 nm in the various concentrations ranging from 5 mg/ml to 25 mg/ml as seen in Fig. 5a). The inset shows a digital photograph displaying bright green emission under a UV-lamp (365 nm). When solid film formation takes place as the composite films are developed with the incorporation of g-CDs into the polymer matrix, luminescent properties are imparted to the composite films. The PL characteristics for the composites appear to deviate from g-CDs. Emission peaks appear to have a broader spectrum with dual bands covering most of the visible region having a sharp peak centered at 475 nm and a broader shoulder peak at 560 nm. In many literature reports wherein CDs are embedded into PVA, such emission peaks are seldom reported. The variation of PL emission intensity with various weight percentages of g-CDs is explained in Fig. 5b) and c).

The emission intensity increases upon increasing the concentration of g-CDs implying that g-CDs alone are responsible for PL emission. Maximum PL intensity is observed for 10 wt% of g-CDs. A more homogenous distribution of g-CDs leads to better PL intensity and the agglomerations affects the PL intensity. The surface states constituted by the functional groups attached to the g-CDs are understood to be capture centres for excitons acting as recombination sites or emissive sites. The presence of these surface states is the key factor responsible for PL emission in addition to size dependent quantum confinement effects in CDs [50]. Change in surface edge happens when the functional groups like –COOH condense with –OH group of PVA. Such a bond formation between g-CDs and PVA induces a strain in the structure of g-CDs and causes changes in surface edge states which constitute new recombination centers that could be responsible for the dual emission peaks observed in the case of composite films. g-CDs concentration beyond 12 wt% leads to a decline in the PL emission. Beyond this concentration, there is saturation in the dispersability causing agglomeration of g-CDs which deteriorates the PL emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH dependence on the PL emission
ce:section.ce:para: The emission properties in an aqueous medium are highly affected by the change in pH of the surrounding medium. The variation of PL intensity with pH of g-CDs in aqueous solution and composite films is depicted in Fig. 6 a) and b) respectively. Significant variation in the PL intensity is observed in the case of g-CDs solution in which pH is varied. In an acidic environment, the surface functional groups attached to g-CDs like carboxyl groups which constitute the emissive sites get protonated and become inactive resulting in a decrease in PL intensity [51]. In alkaline conditions, the reverse happens; these emissive sites become deprotonated which alters the distribution of electron cloud around the functional groups and the carbon backbone, restoring the PL emission. These alterations in the structure of g-CDs in acidic and basic pH conditions are the reasons behind the PL intensity differences observed for g-CDs in an aqueous medium. In the case of g-CDs/PVA composite films, such a considerable change in PL emission is not visible and appears to be unaffected by variations in pH though a slight insignificant variation occurs for the shoulder peak. PVA forms an ideal matrix to host the g-CDs to form freestanding flexible films whose PL emission is not affected by the surrounding environment. In nanocomposite films, g-CDs are firmly packed in the polymer matrix during the transformation of the nanocomposite from its aqueous state to solid film state. This restricts the mobility of g-CDs within the polymer matrix and its deformation. During film formation, hydrogen bonds established between the –OH groups of PVA and –COOH groups of g-CDs retain the anchoring of g-CDs intact within the PVA matrix. Good chemical resistance and strong oxygen barrier properties of PVA also add to protect g-CDs. All these aspects contribute to the observed pH‐independent luminescence emission from g-CDs/PVA nanocomposite films.

Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) thermograms (first heating cycle) and Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) explain the thermal characteristics of g-CDs embedded composite films as in Fig. 7 a) and b) respectively. From 7 a) it is clear that the melting temperature (
T m
) of neat PVA is observed at around 230 °C. In the case of the composite films, the melting temperature slightly shifts to higher temperatures. For instance, the maximum shift is observed for 10 wt% of g-CDs incorporated PVA for which the melting temperature is at 243 °C. The melting enthalpy shows a declining trend as the g-CDs concentration increases. The crystallinity degree (χc) of the composite is defined as the ratio of melting enthalpy (ΔHm) obtained from DSC measurements normalized to the PVA content to the 100 % crystalline melting enthalpy (ΔHo) of the host matrix, i.e,
χ c
=
Δ H m
Δ H 0
.
In the case of PVA, the ΔH0 = 138.6 J/g [52]. The thermal properties are tabulated in Table 1 .

It is noticeable that the crystallinity degree decreases with an increase in g-CDs concentration. This observation is indicative of the fact that g-CDs induce structural disorder in the polymer matrix and act as nanoparticle fillers [53]. Strong molecular interaction like hydrogen bond between PVA and g-CDs could cause structural disorder which reduces the degree of crystallinity.

From the TGA data as in Fig. 7a) the residual water content in the samples is estimated to be ∼ 5–10 % and the removal of water takes place between 50 °C and 150 °C. Embedding g-CDs into the PVA matrix slightly changes the decomposition behavior of the host polymer. The maximum thermal decomposition happens between 250 °C and 380 °C. The maximum weight loss temperature increases from 380 °C for PVA to 448 °C in the case of 10 wt% of g-CDs. Neat PVA decomposes completely before 600 °C whereas carbonized residues (∼15 %) are formed when the composites with higher g-CDs content are decomposed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical characteristics of g-CDs embedded PVA composite films

ce:section.ce:para: The toxicity of nanoparticles is a matter of great concern which restricts their application in chemical sensing, bio-imaging and other relevant technological applications. The cytotoxicity testing indicates the initial step to establish the biocompatibility of the synthesized composites. The pH independent luminescence is highly in favour of bioimaging and chemical sensing application in different pH environment in addition to which the cell-viability studies are equally relevant for in-vivo imaging studies. MTT assay was used to establish the cell viability of the composites in fast growing neuroblastoma cell lines of mouse called N2a cells. Fig. 9 demonstrates the % cell viability of the composite sample (15 wt% g-CDs/PVA) treated cells in comparison to the untreated cells which are known as the control. The result indicates more than 90 % cell viability or cell growth in the composite for various concentrations which is a direct indication of the number of cells remaining after treatment with sample. The result concludes the non-toxic nature of the composite even at high g-CD content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: g-CDs And g-CD/PVA composites as probes for the detection of Pb2+
ce:section.ce:para:
The feasibility of using g-CDs and their polymer composites for the trace level detection of Pb2+ was explored with varying concentration of Pb2+ ions (0––250 µM). There is considerable quenching in the PL emission intensity centered at ∼ 500 nm as the concentration increases from 5 nM to 250 µM as observed in Fig. 10 . The PL quenching was investigated using the Stern-Volmer model [55]

Fig. 11 demonstrates the feasibility of g-CDs/PVA composite as probes for the trace level detection of Pb2+ ions (10 wt% g-CDs). 11(a) shows the quenching pattern in the range 0–40 nM which is linear in nature with

A comparison of this reported value with existing CD based probes is tabulated in supplementary file Table S1. The photographs of the composite films in the presence of different concentrations of Pb2+ under UV excitation are also provided in Fig. S3.

The PL lifetime of g-CDs and the composite films could be calculated from the time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) in which the PL decay exhibit exponential behavior as

This unusually high value is an indication of the fact that the quenching mechanism could rather be a static one than the dynamic quenching as the value of the rate constant for dynamic quenching is usually considered to be less than 1.0 x 1010 M−1 s−1 [56]. The PL life time of g-CD/PVA composite film was found to be 6.93 ns from which the quenching rate constant

For any probe to be used as a sensor, selectivity is an important parameter to be considered alongside sensitivity to realize whether our system is specific towards Pb2+ in an interference of various metal ions. In order to study this, 1 ml of g-CDs solution was mixed with 3 ml of 500 µM of the solution of various metal ions and the PL emission intensity was recorded. The various interfering metal ions such as Fe2+, Fe3+, Na+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ca2+ etc. were considered and it was observed that in the presence of these metal ions the PL emission intensity almost appears to remain unchanged though slight quenching was observed for certain metal ions like Fe3+ and Ni2+ though it was quite negligible compared to Pb2+ ions. A substantial change in the PL emission was observed only upon addition of Pb2+ ions indicative of the fact that the as prepared g-CDs and their polymer composites are highly selective towards Pb2+ ions (Fig. 13 a). The effect of other interfering organic compounds like benzoic acid, toluene, picric acid etc. which are also potential pollutants in water bodies is tested in the presence of Pb2+ ions and PL emission intensity is found to be almost uninterrupted (Fig. 13b). Therefore the use of g-CD/polymer composites for the detection of Pb2+ in complex samples could be proposed.

The stability of the probe was studied by measuring the PL intensity at different timelines (Fig. 14 ). The emission intensity appeared to be unchanged even for a long duration of time, for almost one year of time. Photobleaching is usually observed in the case of fluorescing materials which restrict their practical application in sensing and imaging. Here the stability of the probe for longer period makes them sustainable in the development of portable fluorescent sensors. The emission intensity was also found to be unchanged for different batches of synthesized composite samples. The durability of the composite films in water is also studied and presented (Fig. S2).

The high value of quenching constant
k q
throws some light to the type of quenching observed in the Pb2+ added g-CDs and its composite samples and is found to be static in nature. PL quenching happens due to a variety of molecular interactions between the probe and the quencher like collisional or dynamic quenching due to the collision between fluorophore and the quencher, static or non-fluorescent ground state complex formation between probe and the quencher, fluorescence resonance energy transfer via non-radiative energy transfer between the fluorophore (donor) in its excited state to the quencher (acceptor) through a long range dipole–dipole interactions. The lack of overlap between the emission spectra of g-CDs and the absorption spectra of Pb2+ rules out the possibility for FRET. Moreover the PL lifetime of g-CDs and the composites appear to be almost unaffected even with the addition of Pb2+ ions. The unaffected PL lifetime concludes that the quenching mechanism could be static.

The UV–vis absorption studies and Raman analysis also confirm that the quenching could be caused due to the aggregation of g-CDs in the presence of Pb2+ which is in a way similar to the ground state complex formation of the analyte with the quencher. The surface of the g-CDs is understood to be decorated with carboxylic group (COO–) and this imparts a negative charge on the g-CD surface. The addition of Pb2+ ions nullify the negative charge on the surface of g-CDs which captures the Pb2+ ions by the interaction of g-CDs and carboxyl functionalities existing on the surface causing multiple g-CDs to coordinate with these ions ultimately causing aggregation of g-CDs(Fig. 15 and Fig. S4). A study by Bala et al. also cites the selective affinity of Pb2+ towards carboxylate groups among different divalent metal ions [57]. The UV–vis absorption spectra studies of Pb2+ added g-CDs and the composite films indicate the presence of a broad absorption centered in the visible region between 400 and 500 nm (Fig. 16 a) due to the complexation of Pb2+ ions with carboxylate groups on the g-CDs leading to their aggregation and hence quenching. It is also interesting to note that the absorption maxima of composite films at higher concentrations of Pb2+ occur in the visible region range 380–450 nm (Fig. 16b) and overlaps with emission of the composite films there by causing a quenching of emission and also a slight alteration in the emission spectra of the composite films at higher Pb2+ concentrations as observed in Fig. 11c (beyond 100 µM of Pb2+ ions). It is noteworthy that the UV–vis absorption spectra of g-CDs in the presence of other divalent metal ions does not show any characteristic absorption peaks as observed in Fig. 16c.

The Raman analysis of Pb2+ ions added g-CDs samples also reveal quite interesting results as provided in Fig. 17 a). g-CDs are associated with characteristic D and G peaks located at ∼ 1350 and ∼ 1580 cm−1 respectively. The D peak is attributed to the vibration of C atoms with dangling bonds in terminal plane of disordered carbon which refers to surface defects. The G peak is associated with the vibration of sp2 hybridized C atom in the graphitic domain or the two dimensional hexagonal lattice. Here the surface defects could be referred to as the oxygen rich carboxylate and hydroxyl groups on the surface of g-CDs. The addition of Pb2+ ions leads to a slight shift of peak positions to higher values. The shifts in wavenumber to higher or lower values depend on the alteration in the structure related to changes in vibrational modes. A red shift in the D and G peaks denotes decreased compressive stress and an increase in bond length and lattice parameters of g-CDs upon addition of Pb2+ ions indicative of aggregation of g-CDs. It is also observed that D peak almost dissolves and disappears at higher concentrations of Pb2+ ions due to the aggregation of g-CDs leading to a nature of the spectra analogous to the Raman spectra of sugar derived carbon [58]. From the FTIR analysis (Fig. 17b), the change in intensity and blue shift in the vibrational bands associated with C-O (1040 cm−1), C-H/O-H (1423–1630 cm−1), C = O (1700 cm−1), broadening of C-H stretching mode (2880–2900 cm−1) characteristic of carboxyl moieties ascertain that the attachment of Pb2+ ions occur with the carboxylate group. The absence of new vibrational peaks indicates that there is no covalent bond formation between g-CDs and the quencher ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of g-CD/PVA composites’ sensing efficiency in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: A validation of the probes for the trace level detection of Pb2+ ions in real water samples was tested using water collected from nearby water sources like bore well, tap water and lake (Vembanad lake, Kochi). All the three samples were syringe filtered before use and spiked with varying concentrations of Pb2+ ions. These spiked samples were added to the probe (10 wt% g-CDs/PVA films) and subjected to Pb2+ ions detection by recording the emission response. The values of added and found concentrations by the method of standard addition are tabulated as in Table 4 . The obtained values indicate that our probe offer good detection and recovery rate of Pb2+ in real water samples.

g-CDs developed from glucose are successfully integrated into a benign polymer matrix PVA via a facile solution casting method with water as solvent to form flexible freestanding g-CDs/PVA composite films. No surface modication or addition of surfactant was required to embed g-CDs into the matrix and g-CD/PVA showed interesting PL emission properties. This approach offers a feasible approach to protect g-CDs from working environment and provides good chemical, thermal and mechanical stability. The PL emission was broad covering almost the entire region of visible spectrum with sharp peak at 470 nm and broad one cantered at 560 nm where as g-CDs showed an emission peak in the green region centered at ∼ 512 nm. These composite films were used as probes for the trace level detection of Pb2+ in water based on PL quenching induced in the presence of these metal ions. The limit of detection is as low as 0.93 nM and 4.7 nM for g-CD/PVA composites and g-CDs respectively with its efficacy tested in real water samples. The probe is also found to be highly selective towards Pb2+. The detection mechanism is found to be static from PL lifetime studies, UV–vis absorption and Raman analysis as it was observed that Pb2+ gets attached to the carboxylate groups on g-CDs in the PVA matrix which initiates the aggregation of g-CDs and hence a PL quenching. These studies provide ample scope for the development of flexible, freestanding polymer films as sustainable probes for the on-site strip based testing of lead and hence in the environmental monitoring of heavy metal ion pollution in water.
